sees only the final value after the window expires. This verifies the two flows
is a hot stream that holds state and multicasts to all collectors.
Testing it requires collecting in a background task before sending updates, because
the stream only replays the current value at subscription time.

abstract.text: values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.
Real sleeps make tests slow and flaky. FlowKit ships a
virtual clock, and a suite of
helpers that let you assert on stream values
values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.

content: // TestClock starts at virtual time zero. Calling advance(by:) moves
// the clock forward without any real time passing.
// TestScope.run collects the flow in a concurrent background task so
// that advance() calls interleave correctly with the debounce sleeps.
// Small real-time yield lets the collection task attach before we emit.
// Assertions continue in the next steps...

abstract.text: Real sleeps make tests slow and flaky. FlowKit ships a
virtual clock, and a suite of
helpers that let you assert on stream values
Authentication state is the classic cross-cutting concern: the home
tab, the profile screen, and a UIKit sign-in controller all need to
react when the user signs in or out. Traditional approaches like singletons
a reactive stream every layer of your app can observe independently.
Search-as-you-type is a great user experience, but firing a network
request on every keystroke wastes bandwidth and hammers your server.
A debounced pipeline collapses rapid keystrokes into a single request
that fires only after the user pauses. FlowKit makes this a four-operator

content: @Test("raw query and debounced query are independent")
// Both flows are collected concurrently, each in its own background task.
// Assertions against both testers continue in the next step.

abstract.text: Learn FlowKit by building real features. These tutorials walk through
the patterns that come up most often: search-as-you-type, shared state
across screens, wrapping callback APIs, and writing deterministic tests.
Authentication state is the classic cross-cutting concern: the home
tab, the profile screen, and a UIKit sign-in controller all need to
react when the user signs in or out. Traditional approaches like singletons
a reactive stream every layer of your app can observe independently.
Real sleeps make tests slow and flaky. FlowKit ships a
virtual clock, and a suite of
helpers that let you assert on stream values

content: // MutableStateFlow replays its current value to each new collector,
// so the first expectValue call sees the initial state immediately.
// The initial value is emitted as soon as the collector subscribes.
// Assertions for transitions continue in the next step.

content: // MutableStateFlow emits the new value to all collectors.
// ThrowingFlow uses ThrowingFlowTester which adds expectError(_:).
// Typed overload that works when the error type is Equatable.
// Simulate three rapid keystrokes 100 ms apart.
// 300 ms total elapsed, but the last keystroke reset the timer
// 100 ms ago, so we are only 100 ms into the 300 ms window.
// Nothing should have been emitted yet.
@Test("collapses rapid inputs into a single emission")
// Nothing yet. Still inside the 300 ms silence window.
// Advance past the debounce window. The clock wakes the sleeping
// debounce task, which emits the last value ("swi").
// No further values. Intermediate "s" and "sw" were suppressed.
// Sending the same state again must NOT produce a new emission.

content: @Test("debounced pipeline emits once after window expires")
// Rapid typing, each keystroke resets the debounce timer.
// Still inside the window, so no results yet.
// Expire the window. The pipeline fires a single search.
// Predicate overload, useful when the error has associated values
// you want to inspect without requiring Equatable conformance.
try await tester.expectError("invalid query error") { error in
guard case SearchError.invalidQuery(let q) = error else { return false }
